scenarios.situation: Priya just shipped a big feature. In standup she lit up talking about it and clearly wants the team to notice. How do you respond so she feels recognized?

clues.plain: Priya is energized by people and loves a bit of the spotlight.
She's motivated by visible wins and public recognition.

options.text: Give her a quiet, private thank-you note later.
Call out her win in front of the whole team and let her share how she did it.
Send a detailed written breakdown of what she could improve.
Say nothing — the work speaks for itself.

scenarios.whyCorrect: Priya's drive is social and recognition-seeking (High Extraversion). Public credit + a chance to talk about it lands perfectly.
scenarios.whyWrongCommon: A private note isn't wrong-hearted, but it misses what energizes her — she wants the room to see it.
scenarios.facilitatorPrompt: Who on your real team lights up with public recognition, and who would rather get it quietly?

scenarios.situation: Marcus snapped at the team in today's standup, then went silent for the rest of the meeting. How do you address it?

clues.plain: Marcus likes things done exactly right and hates feeling rushed.
Big public confrontation drains him; he opens up one-on-one.

options.text: Raise it in the next team meeting so everyone can clear the air.
Pull him aside privately, later, with a calm and specific plan.
Let it blow over — don't bring it up at all.
Send a quick all-team message reminding everyone to stay positive.

scenarios.whyCorrect: Low Extraversion means a public call-out backfires; High Formality means he needs a clear, structured plan. A private, prepared one-on-one fits both.
scenarios.whyWrongCommon: Clearing the air in the room feels fair, but it's exactly the public confrontation that shuts him down.
scenarios.facilitatorPrompt: When someone's upset, does your team default to addressing it publicly or privately?

scenarios.situation: Dana keeps reorganizing the team's shared process and pushing for faster decisions. A couple of teammates feel steamrolled. How do you channel her?

clues.plain: Dana is driven to take charge and make things happen fast.
She gets impatient with slow, repetitive routines.

options.text: Give her ownership of a fast-moving project where her drive helps.
Ask her to slow down and just follow the existing routine.
Put her on a long, detailed, repetitive maintenance task.
Tell her to stop making changes without asking everyone first.

scenarios.whyCorrect: High Dominance + Low Patience needs an outlet — give her a fast, high-ownership challenge and the steamrolling turns into momentum.
scenarios.whyWrongCommon: Forcing her to slow down and follow routine fights her drives head-on and usually backfires.
scenarios.facilitatorPrompt: Where could a high-drive, fast-moving person on your team be pointed so it's an asset?

scenarios.situation: A big workflow change is coming. Sam, usually reliable and calm, has gone quiet and seems anxious about it. How do you bring Sam along?

clues.plain: Sam values stability and a steady, predictable pace.
Sam feels safe with clear rules and knowing exactly what's expected.

options.text: Spring the change quickly and ask Sam to just adapt on the fly.
Give Sam plenty of notice, a clear step-by-step plan, and time to adjust.
Tell Sam to stop worrying — change is good.
Make Sam the one to announce the change to everyone else.

scenarios.whyCorrect: High Patience needs time and warning; High Formality needs structure and clarity. A clear plan with notice settles both.
scenarios.whyWrongCommon: Springing it on Sam to 'just adapt' hits exactly the two things that create anxiety here.
scenarios.facilitatorPrompt: How does your team usually roll out change — and who needs more lead time than others?

scenarios.situation: Jordan is brilliant but won't delegate — taking on everything personally and starting to burn out. The team feels underused. How do you intervene?

clues.plain: Jordan wants control and to do things their own way.
Jordan is also a perfectionist about getting details exactly right.
Jordan is reserved and doesn't naturally talk through their workload.

options.text: Order Jordan to hand off half their tasks immediately.
Privately co-create a clear delegation plan with defined quality standards Jordan helps set.
Announce in the team meeting that Jordan needs to delegate more.
Leave Jordan alone — they'll ask for help when they need it.

scenarios.whyCorrect: The conflict: High Dominance + High Formality means Jordan won't let go unless quality is guaranteed and they keep control of the standards — and Low Extraversion means it has to be private. Co-creating the plan, privately, with Jordan setting the bar, respects all three.
scenarios.whyWrongCommon: Ordering them to hand off tasks ignores the need for control and quality — they'll quietly take the work back.
scenarios.facilitatorPrompt: Is there a 'won't let go' person on your team? What would make it safe for them to delegate?

scenarios.situation: Two teammates clash constantly: Alex wants to move fast and decide now; Robin wants to slow down and get every detail right. Meetings stall. How do you get them working together?

clues.plain: Alex is driven to act fast and push decisions forward.
Robin needs accuracy, structure, and time to do it correctly.

options.text: Pick a side — tell them the company moves fast, so Robin needs to keep up.
Give them distinct roles that play to each drive: Alex drives momentum/decisions, Robin owns quality/review — with an agreed handoff.
Force them to compromise in the middle on everything.
Separate them so they never have to work together.

scenarios.whyCorrect: Their drives aren't a flaw — they're complementary. Defining roles so each owns the part that fits them (Alex = momentum, Robin = accuracy) turns the clash into a strength.
scenarios.whyWrongCommon: Splitting the difference frustrates both; picking a side wastes one person's real strength.
scenarios.facilitatorPrompt: Where do opposite working styles on your team actually complement each other if framed right?

scenarios.situation: Taylor is warm and persuasive with clients but keeps skipping the CRM admin and follow-up paperwork. It's causing dropped balls. How do you fix it without killing their strength?

clues.plain: Taylor thrives on people, talking, and winning others over.
Taylor finds detailed rules and repetitive admin draining.

options.text: Pair Taylor with a detail-oriented teammate (or tool) to handle the admin, and let Taylor focus on relationships.
Put Taylor on a strict paperwork-compliance improvement plan.
Take Taylor off client work until the admin is caught up.
Just keep reminding Taylor to do the paperwork.

scenarios.whyCorrect: High Extraversion + Low Formality means admin will always be the weak spot — don't fight it, support it. Pairing or automating the detail work keeps Taylor's real strength (relationships) firing.
scenarios.whyWrongCommon: A compliance plan targets the exact thing their drives resist; it rarely sticks and dents morale.
scenarios.facilitatorPrompt: Whose strengths are you accidentally blocking by making them do their weakest type of work?

scenarios.situation: Casey is the dependable one who quietly keeps everything running, but never speaks up in meetings and is being overlooked for a lead role. How do you draw Casey out and recognize them?

clues.plain: Casey is steady, patient, and supportive — the team's anchor.
Casey is reserved and won't self-promote in a group.

options.text: Put Casey on the spot in the next meeting to share their accomplishments.
Ask for Casey's input privately first, then credit their contributions specifically and visibly to the group.
Wait for Casey to step up and ask for the lead role.
Give the lead role to someone louder who clearly wants it.

scenarios.whyCorrect: Low Extraversion + Low Dominance means Casey won't self-advocate — so you surface their value for them: gather input privately, then give specific public credit. Recognition without forcing the spotlight.
scenarios.whyWrongCommon: Putting Casey on the spot to brag is the opposite of what a reserved person can do in the moment — it freezes them.
scenarios.facilitatorPrompt: Who's the quiet anchor on your team that might be getting overlooked?

scenarios.situation: A founder, River, makes fast unilateral calls and resists process — it worked at 5 people but now at 30 the team feels whiplash and out of the loop. How do you help River adapt without crushing the drive that built the company?

clues.plain: River is highly independent, decisive, and comfortable with risk.
River moves fast and gets impatient with process and routine.

options.text: Tell River to stop deciding things alone and run everything through the team.
Keep River as the fast decision-maker but add a lightweight 'here's what I decided and why' broadcast so the team isn't blindsided.
Implement a heavy formal approval process for all decisions.
Accept the whiplash — it's just how founders are.

scenarios.whyCorrect: You don't kill High Dominance/Low Formality/Low Patience by forcing heavy process — you add the lightest possible structure (a quick decision broadcast) that keeps River fast while giving the team visibility.
scenarios.whyWrongCommon: A heavy approval process directly fights every one of River's drives — they'll route around it and resent it.

scenarios.situation: New hire Noor is technically sharp but hasn't said a word in three team meetings and seems hesitant to ask questions. The team reads it as disinterest. How do you integrate Noor?

clues.plain: Noor is reserved and processes things internally before speaking.
Noor wants to be sure they're correct before contributing.

options.text: Call on Noor unexpectedly in meetings to force participation.
Send Noor topics in advance and offer a written or one-on-one channel to contribute.
Assume Noor isn't a fit and start looking elsewhere.
Tell Noor they need to speak up more if they want to succeed.

scenarios.whyCorrect: Low Extraversion + High Formality means Noor contributes best with prep time and a lower-pressure channel — not on-the-spot. Give advance notice and a written option and the insight flows.
scenarios.whyWrongCommon: Cold-calling a reserved, get-it-right person is the fastest way to make them shut down harder.
scenarios.facilitatorPrompt: How could your meetings give prep time so quieter people can contribute their best?
